text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - SURVEILLANCE VISIT
Head and Neck Oncology Program

ATTENDING: [Medical Oncology Attending]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine post-treatment surveillance visit. Patient with HPV-positive (p16+) right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma, T2N1M0, status post definitive concurrent chemoradiation, here for 6-month post-treatment follow-up.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old Chinese-American never-smoker and high school music teacher who was diagnosed approximately 8 months ago with Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition) HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue. She underwent definitive concurrent chemoradiation: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) using a simultaneous integrated boost technique delivering 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-dose planning target volume (primary tumor and involved right level IIA node), with concurrent cis 100 mg/m2 IV on days 1 and 22. She completed the full planned 35-fraction course without treatment interruptions, receiving both planned cycles of cis at full dose.

Her treatment course was notable for grade 3 radiation mucositis during weeks 5-7, which required percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement for nutritional support. She also experienced grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the bilateral neck, grade 1-2 xerostomia, and grade 2 fatigue. Post-treatment PET/CT obtained at approximately 12 weeks post-completion demonstrated complete metabolic response - no FDG-avid disease at the primary site or regional lymph nodes. This was confirmed on flexible nasopharyngoscopy, which showed no residual tumor at the base of tongue.

She presents today approximately 6 months after completion of chemoradiation. She reports a meaningful improvement in quality of life compared to the acute treatment period. She has resumed part-time work as a music teacher, which she notes is gratifying and important to her emotional recovery. She is able to eat a soft mechanical diet and is tolerating thin liquids. She underwent PEG tube removal by gastroenterology approximately one month ago, after demonstrating that she was meeting greater than 85% of her daily caloric needs orally. She endorses persistent dry mouth, which is bothersome particularly while teaching, but reports that the pilocarpine initiated last month has provided some partial relief. She denies new throat pain, new neck lumps, hemoptysis, otalgia, odynophagia at rest, or unexplained weight loss. No fevers, chills, or constitutional symptoms.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Persistent xerostomia (stable, grade 1-2); mild fatigue with prolonged activity; occasional difficulty with dry or chewy food textures.
Negative: Denies pain at rest in the throat, neck, or ear; no new neck masses; no hemoptysis or blood-tinged sputum; no trismus; no voice change from post-treatment baseline; no nasal obstruction; no visual changes; no dyspnea; no new constitutional symptoms. No nausea, vomiting, or abdominal complaints since PEG tube removal. No lower extremity edema.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: Blood pressure within normal limits. Heart rate regular. Weight: [recorded - net deficit approximately 2 lbs from pre-treatment baseline, continuing to recover]. Performance status: ECOG 0.

General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and conversant. Mood good.

Head/Neck:
- Oropharynx: Well-healed mucosa. Mild residual erythema of the posterior oropharynx consistent with post-radiation changes. No discrete mass or ulceration visible on direct oral exam. Tongue base not fully visualized without endoscopy - see below.
- Salivary glands: Submandibular glands palpably normal. No parotid masses. Mouth clearly dry on oral inspection consistent with grade 1 xerostomia.
- Dentition: Intact. Tooth extraction site (tooth #19) completely healed.
- Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No palpable masses. Bilateral neck skin with residual hyperpigmentation in prior radiation field; no moist desquamation; no induration suggestive of significant fibrosis.
- Flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed in clinic: Nasal cavity and nasopharynx clear. Oropharynx: mucosa well-healed at the right base of tongue, no visible mass, no ulceration, no abnormal vascular pattern. Bilateral valleculae clear. Supraglottis and glottis unremarkable. Vocal cord mobility normal bilaterally.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. PEG tube site well-healed, no erythema or granulation tissue.
Extremities: No edema. No peripheral neuropathy on gross exam.

---

LABORATORY / DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS
Recent laboratory data reviewed:
- CBC: Within normal limits. No cytopenias.
- BMP: Electrolytes normal. Creatinine 0.85 mg/dL (stable from baseline; no significant nephrotoxicity from cis). eGFR >80 mL/min.
- TSH: 5.8 mIU/L (elevated above normal range). Free T4 low-normal at 0.9 ng/dL. Consistent with radiation-induced hypothyroidism superimposed on pre-existing hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine.

Imaging: Most recent surveillance PET/CT (12 weeks post-treatment) demonstrated complete metabolic response with no FDG-avid disease at the primary site (right base of tongue; previously SUVmax 12.4, now background activity) or at the right level IIA lymph node (previously SUVmax 8.7). No distant metastatic disease. Today no new imaging is being ordered; 12-month post-treatment CT neck with contrast planned per surveillance protocol.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-POSITIVE (p16+) RIGHT BASE OF TONGUE SCC, T2N1M0, STATUS POST DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CHEMORADIATION - COMPLETE RESPONSE, NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE
Ms. Chen is doing well at the 6-month post-treatment mark. Clinical examination is reassuring: no palpable adenopathy, no endoscopic evidence of recurrent or residual tumor at the primary site. Her 12-week post-treatment PET/CT confirmed complete metabolic response. She is counseled that HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC carries an excellent prognosis, particularly in patients achieving complete response after definitive chemoradiation - 5-year overall survival rates in her population are in the range of 85% or better. No adjuvant systemic therapy is indicated per current standard of care for complete responders.
- Continue NCCN-based HPV-positive OPSCC surveillance: H&P every 1-3 months during years 1-2. Next visit in 3 months.
- CT neck with contrast planned at 12 months post-treatment completion (approximately 6 months from today); PET/CT to be reserved for equivocal findings per clinical judgment.
- Patient counseled to present urgently if new neck mass, persistent throat pain, hemoptysis, otalgia, or unexplained weight loss develop.

2. XEROSTOMIA, GRADE 1 - POST-RADIATION, CHRONIC
Expected late effect of parotid gland irradiation (mean parotid doses: right 28 Gy, left 24 Gy). Patient partially responding to pilocarpine 5 mg TID initiated last visit.
- Continue pilocarpine 5 mg TID; counsel on staying well hydrated. If GI side effects intolerable, can trial dose reduction to 5 mg BID.
- Continue artificial saliva spray (e.g., Biotene) as needed, particularly for teaching and social engagements.
- Reinforce daily fluoride tray use given xerostomia-related increased risk of dental caries. Dental surveillance every 6 months.

3. DYSPHAGIA - IMPROVING, POST-RADIATION
Patient tolerating soft mechanical diet and thin liquids, meeting nutritional needs entirely by mouth since PEG removal. Some difficulty with dry, chewy, or sticky food textures persists.
- Continue outpatient speech-language pathology for dysphagia rehabilitation; goal of advancing to regular diet.
- Encourage Mendelsohn maneuver and supraglottic swallow techniques as instructed by SLP.
- Monitor weight at each visit; goal return to within 1-2 lbs of pre-treatment baseline.

4. RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM
TSH now elevated at 5.8 mIU/L with low-normal free T4, on background of pre-existing hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. The thyroid gland sits within the prior radiation field. Radiation thyroiditis/hypothyroidism is a well-recognized late effect.
- Levothyroxine dose increased today (specific dose per pharmacy record). Recheck TSH and free T4 in 6 weeks.
- Long-term thyroid surveillance: TSH annually, or more frequently as needed to maintain euthyroid status.

5. Platinol-RELATED LATE EFFECTS MONITORING
- Nephrotoxicity: Creatinine remains at baseline (0.85 mg/dL); no significant renal impairment. Continue annual BMP monitoring.
- Ototoxicity: Audiogram to be repeated at 12-month visit per institutional protocol (2 cycles of high-dose Platinol). Mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss is anticipated but not yet formally documented post-treatment; patient does not endorse subjective hearing complaints at this time.
- Patient counseled on long-term ototoxicity risk in her occupation (music teacher) and to avoid recreational noise exposure without hearing protection.

6. LATE RADIATION EFFECTS - GENERAL COUNSELING
- Carotid stenosis: Counseled that irradiation of the bilateral neck may be associated with carotid atherosclerosis in the long term. Discussed importance of cardiovascular risk factor management (she is a never-smoker, favorable profile). Carotid ultrasound or clinical monitoring per cardiology/vascular surgery referral if symptoms arise.
- Neck skin: Residual post-radiation hyperpigmentation. Reinforce daily broad-spectrum SPF 50 sunscreen for neck and face skin.
- Trismus prevention: No significant trismus at present; encourage jaw-opening exercises as previously instructed.
- Osteoradionecrosis: Low ongoing risk given tooth #19 prophylactic extraction prior to RT. Dental hygiene and early reporting of jaw pain emphasized.

Return to clinic in approximately 3 months for continued surveillance. Patient to follow up with radiation oncology (Dr. Park) and ENT/head and neck surgery per respective schedules. Speech-language pathology and dental oncology follow-up ongoing. Patient understands and is agreeable to the plan.

[Electronically signed by attending medical oncologist]
